output: "{\"contexts\":[\"[COST] Park and library events: Events organized by city parks departments and public library systems are almost always genuinely free. These are taxpayer-funded services designed for public access. Story times, park concerts, nature walks led by rangers, and library workshops are reliable free events.\",\"[COST] Museum free days and hours: Many museums offer free admission on specific days or during certain hours (e.g., first Friday free, free after 4pm). These are genuinely free. However, special exhibitions, planetarium shows, or IMAX screenings within the museum may still cost extra even on free days.\",\"[COST] Ticketed free events: Some free events require tickets for capacity management. If the ticket is $0 with no fees, this is genuinely free. However, some platforms charge processing fees even for free tickets ($1-3). Check whether 'free tickets' have any associated fees before confirming as truly free.\",\"[COST] Free with registration caveats: Some events listed as 'free' require registration through platforms that add service fees, or require purchasing a separate item (like a book for a book signing, or supplies for a craft class). 'Free admission' may not mean free participation if materials or extras cost money.\",\"[COST] Eventbrite free event nuances: On Eventbrite, events can be listed as 'free' while having paid add-ons, VIP tiers, or required purchases. The 'free' label on Eventbrite means the base ticket is $0, but the actual event experience may involve costs. Check the full event description for hidden charges.\",\"[COST] Free trial and promotional events: Events framed as 'free first class,' 'free trial session,' or 'introductory offer' are marketing funnels for paid services. While technically free to attend, they exist to convert attendees into paying customers. Fitness studios, martial arts dojos, and membership-based organizations commonly use this model.\",\"[COST] Meetup group event costs: Meetup.com groups sometimes charge event fees or require venue minimums. A board game night at a cafe may require a purchase. A hiking meetup is free, but a cooking class meetup may have ingredient fees. The platform listing as free does not guarantee the event itself is free.\",\"[COST] Volunteer events and hidden expectations: Volunteer events are free to participate in but sometimes expect financial contributions (like paying for your own supplies, transportation, or a 'volunteer fee' for international programs). Local park cleanups, habitat builds, and food bank volunteering are typically genuinely free. Check for any required purchases or donations.\"],\"answer\":\"Events evaluated: 3\\nPassed: Art Museum Free Day\\nRejected: Free Outdoor Movie Night, Yoga & Brunch Workshop\\n\\nPASS [0.90] Art Museum Free Day: This appears to be a genuine museum free day with no mentioned costs, fees, or suggested donations.\\nREJECT [0.30] Free Outdoor Movie Night: While admission is free, the event explicitly mentions a '$5 suggested donation for popcorn' which violates the no suggested donations criterion.\\nREJECT [0.20] Yoga & Brunch Workshop: While the yoga portion may be free, the event includes a $25 brunch component, making it not genuinely free overall.\",\"filteredTitles\":[\"Art Museum Free Day\"],\"rejectedTitles\":[\"Free Outdoor Movie Night\",\"Yoga & Brunch Workshop\"]}"

output: "{\"contexts\":[\"[FAMILY] Genuinely family-friendly signals: Events that explicitly mention 'all ages,' 'bring the kids,' 'family-friendly,' children's activities, face painting, bounce houses, or kid-specific programming are designed for families. Events at children's museums, playgrounds, or family recreation centers are inherently family-oriented.\",\"[FAMILY] Content and theme indicators: Events involving comedy shows (which may include adult humor), horror-themed events, haunted houses for adults, burlesque, drag brunches, or events with explicit content warnings are not family-friendly. Look for age recommendations, content warnings, or adult-oriented themes in descriptions.\",\"[FAMILY] Age-appropriate timing: Events starting after 8pm or marketed for 'adults only,' '21+,' or '18+' are not family-friendly by definition. Late-night events, even those not explicitly age-restricted, are impractical for families with young children. Daytime and early evening events are more likely family-appropriate.\",\"[FAMILY] Bar and nightclub family events: Some bars and nightclubs host daytime 'family-friendly' events like brunch or afternoon activities. While technically open to children, the venue is primarily designed for adult entertainment and drinking. The environment may not be truly appropriate for families despite the labeling.\",\"[FAMILY] Pet-friendly vs family-friendly: Pet-friendly events (dog park meetups, pet adoption events) are often family-friendly but not always. Large off-leash dog events may not be safe for toddlers. Events at dog bars or pet-friendly breweries combine animal activities with alcohol venues. Evaluate the venue independently from the pet-friendly label.\",\"[FAMILY] Sports events and family safety: Professional sporting events can be family-friendly in terms of content but involve alcohol sales, rowdy crowds, and late endings. Minor league games, community sports events, and youth leagues are more reliably family-friendly. College games vary depending on the tailgating culture.\",\"[GENERAL] Multi-day festival filtering: Multi-day festivals and fairs often have mixed programming — some family-friendly daytime activities and adult-oriented evening events. Do not assume the entire festival matches a single filter. Individual sessions or time slots within a festival should be evaluated separately.\",\"[GENERAL] Seasonal event patterns: Summer brings more outdoor events (concerts in parks, festivals, farmers markets). Winter has more indoor events (holiday markets, craft fairs, indoor fitness). Spring and fall are transition periods with both. Seasonal expectations help identify events that seem out of place for the time of year.\"],\"answer\":\"Events evaluated: 2\\nPassed: Kids Science Discovery Day\\nRejected: Stand-Up Comedy Night\\n\\nPASS [0.95] Kids Science Discovery Day: This event is explicitly designed for children with hands-on activities at a children's museum, making it genuinely family-friendly and appropriate for all ages.\\nREJECT [0.00] Stand-Up Comedy Night: This event is explicitly marked as 21+ only with uncensored adult humor and a drink minimum, making it completely inappropriate for families with children.\",\"filteredTitles\":[\"Kids Science Discovery Day\"],\"rejectedTitles\":[\"Stand-Up Comedy Night\"]}"
